        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "```markdown\n# TURNING: GLASS · BOOK N01  \n**“The Grip That Holds”**  \n\n---\n\n## Chapter One – A Door That Must Never Sag\n\n**Third Casting, Anneal of Molten Settling, Crucible Two**  \n*Rue of Saint Merro, Outer Crown district*\n\nJhace wiped iron filings from his palms and studied the commission slip. The paper had Guild weight, but the specifications were not standard.  \n\n*Hinge must carry river-door span of three arm-lengths, bear load of moving water without sag, accommodate settling of wall that leans ten fingers off plumb.*  \n\nNo alloy specified. No budget ceiling. No formal timeline. Just a small note at the bottom: *Make it move like she stands.* The signature was a single letter. *T. Merrow*.\n\nHe folded the slip into his apron and shouldered his tool-roll.\n\nThe Rue of Saint Merro was a narrow cobbled cut between tall brick buildings that leaned south as if the whole district had slipped a little and never been set right.  \n\nThe sun here came near straight down at midsummer, with no polite angle to soften it. Saint Merro sat close enough to the world’s middle line that seasons were mostly a matter of rain and river height, not cold and snow. Guild maps called this band the Midline Crown. To Jhace it was just the place where walls sweated in high Casting and never quite dried.\n\nMerrow Glass & Mirrors held the ground floor of a four-storey block whose tilt everyone had stopped commenting on years ago. For window frames and mirrors, the lean was a nuisance but manageable. For a river-door—a moving pane that had to seal against water on one side and air on the other—it was a failure waiting to happen.\n\nThe shop door stood open. Inside, heat rolled out in steady waves.\n\nTiffani Merrow stood at a worktable with both hands in a tub of sand and water. She did not look up when he entered. Her spine was straight, knees loose, weight centered over her feet. He could see her feeling the mix by pressure and drag, judging how much lime had burned away in the last firing.\n\n“You're the hinge-maker,” she said.\n\n“Jhace Torrin.” He set his tool-roll on a clear patch of bench. “Your slip doesn't ask for a hinge. It asks for something that doesn't exist yet.”\n\n“The wall leans ten fingers.” She wiped her hands on her trousers and turned toward him. Heat had flushed her cheeks; her eyes were clear and flat. “The door can't. Either you make metal that acts like the stone should, or I tear down three floors of brick I can't afford to rebuild.”\n\nShe led him through racks of glass to the back wall. The river-door frame stood there, propped against temporary supports. Three arm-lengths of divided light: clear panes set in lead, faint ripples caught from the last pour. The lower rail already showed a slight bow where the building pressed down.\n\n“How long has it been leaning?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Since the last Tide-turn,” she said. “Foundation mortar failed. Half this side of the district settled two fingers. Everything above that pretended not to notice.”\n\nShe tapped the wall. The sound was dull.\n\n“This is supposed to be dead load,” she said. “It moves. Any ordinary hinge will chew the glass to pieces in a season.”\n\nJhace knelt at the threshold. The stone was cool under his fingers. Warm air from the furnace slid over it, bringing up the smell of ash and lime.\n\nBehind him, she went back to the sand. Grain hissed against grain as she sifted.\n\n“Your furnace runs hot,” he said.\n\n“Always,” she answered. “I anneal at lemon. Hold until the color softens. If I rush it, the glass remembers and cracks a year later.”\n\nHe watched her hands: even scoop, even shake, tap-tap to settle. No wasted lift in the shoulders. She let the building’s lean travel through her joints instead of resisting it in one place.\n\n“How long do you hold at lemon?” he asked.\n\n“Sixty heartbeats. Longer if the pane is thick.” She nodded toward him. “And you?”\n\n“Forty to blood, ninety to full cherry,” he said. “Past that, the iron forgets what I meant it to be.”\n\nShe met his eyes. “Then you know what I need. A hinge that counts with the building instead of fighting it.”\n\nJhace took a charcoal stub from his apron and, without asking, drew on the bare wood beside the frame.\n\n“Three leaves,” he said. “Two will bind when the wall shifts. Three gives you a bit of float.” He sketched the overlapping plates, each pin offset. “Center leaf takes the drive. Outer leaves guide. Here—” he marked the middle “—a saddle-pin that can slide a finger’s width as the wall settles.”\n\nShe watched his hand, not the drawing. “And when the wall settles more than a finger?”\n\n“Then you brace again,” he said. “I’m not a mason. I can give you room to breathe, not a new foundation.”\n\n“How many days?” she asked.\n\n“Three for pattern, four for forging and fit,” he said automatically. “Seven.”\n\n“The Guild wants this door sealed by the next Tide,” she said. “They think the river will come up through the cellars if we don’t.”\n\n“The Guild thinks a lot and lifts little.” Jhace wiped charcoal on his apron. “I’ll have it in seven days. If it doesn’t do what I say, I’ll melt it down and start again.”\n\nShe held out a hand. He took it. Her grip was firm and exact, the way she handled tools. Callus along the outer edge from bracing against a vise, small burn scars scattered across the back.\n\n“One more thing,” she said. “The wall won’t stop leaning. Don’t make a hinge that tries to pull it straight. Make one that can live with it.”\n\nHe tucked that away with the rest.\n\nOn the walk back to the forge, he ran the commission through his head. Lean, load, span, water pressure, unknown future settlement. By the time he lit the coals and unrolled his tools, the pattern in his mind was already shifting from sketch to metal.\n\nHe set slate on the bench and began to draw. The lines came out with more curve than he expected, a slight bias that matched the wall’s direction. He didn’t correct it. He let his hand follow the memory of how she had stood in her own workshop, weight steady while everything around her leaned.\n\nThe hinge, he decided, would do the same. It would not fix the world. It would simply refuse to sag with it.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Two – Pressure Lines\n\n**Fifth Casting, Anneal of Setting Glass, Crucible Two**  \n*Merrow Glass & Mirrors, Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nTiffani closed the ledger. The last entry sat in red ink: *Merrow Glass flagged for review. Foundation unstable. Deliveries inconsistent. Recommend consolidation.*  \n\nThe Guild liked tidy lists. They did not like tilted lines.\n\n“Should I stay?” Marc stood by the furnace with a polishing cloth in one hand and a smear of cerium on his cheek. His hair stuck to his forehead in damp curls.\n\n“Go home,” Tiffani said. “Your mother needs you at the well. I’ll finish the transom.”\n\n“You always say that, then you’re here when the morning bread carts come.” He hesitated anyway. “The river-door—”\n\n“Needs a hinge first.” She didn’t look up. “Seven days. Go.”\n\nHe heard the tone and didn’t push. “Yes, mistress.”\n\nThe apron came off. Boots thudded down the stairs. The front door scraped shut. She listened for his footsteps on the street until they blended with the rest of the Rue.\n\nSilence took a different shape when the last other person left. The furnace still breathed. Glass still ticked as it cooled. But the small noises that belonged to Marc—off-beat taps, muttered counting—were gone.\n\nShe rose and walked the length of the workshop.\n\nFurnace at one end, banked to a steady orange. Tool bench with rods and paddles laid out in their usual order. Sacks of sand stacked along the wall. The river-door frame near the back, leaning against the brick that should have been fixed and was not.\n\nShe knelt by the threshold stone and pressed her palm to it. The granite was cool. The vibration beneath it was not imagined. A faint, steady tremor moved through the slab, up into her bones, as if something deep below the foundations had started to grind.\n\nThe building had been leaning for years. Lately, the lean felt less like an old habit and more like a decision.\n\nShe stood and checked the nearest pane for strain, lifting it to the light. The glass quivered in her hands. Her grip was firm. The movement came from within the sheet itself, a tiny ripple running across it as if it wanted to flex.\n\nShe set it down and frowned.\n\nHeat pressed against her skin as she opened the furnace door. Lemon light spilled out. She fed the fire, closed the draft, and checked the next pane.\n\nThe ringing started then. High, thin, almost too sharp to make sense of. It cut through the furnace noise, through the street noise, and drew a line straight behind her eyes.\n\nShe held still.\n\nNot the furnace. Its sound was lower and rougher. No wagon rattled by. No one dragged metal on stone. The note hung in place.\n\nIt grew—not louder in the room, but closer to wherever her hearing decided the world ended.\n\nHer teeth began to ache. Copper rose on her tongue.\n\n“The kiln?” she said under her breath, more to give the sound a name than from belief.\n\nShe checked anyway. The bricks sat steady. No new cracks. The flue did not rattle.\n\nThe note climbed until it stopped being a clean tone. Her skin prickled. The hairs on her arms lifted.\n\nOn the racks, a few glass sheets trembled. Not enough to knock against each other. Just enough for the light to slide across them in strange ways.\n\n“Stop,” she said.\n\nThey did not.\n\nSomething deeper joined the high line—a low vibration she felt more in her chest than in her ears. The floor picked it up first. The boards under her boots buzzed as if the new hum were coming up from the stone below, not down from the roof.\n\nHer heart tried to match the rhythm and fell out of step.\n\nA small pane cut for the river-door cracked with a sharp, single sound. A pattern of lines ran across it in one instant.\n\nShe flinched.\n\n“Enough,” she said. The word came out tight.\n\nNothing changed.\n\nThe pressure in her ears thickened. The air tasted like hot stone and metal more than smoke.\n\nShe took one step toward the window. The floor accepted it half a beat late. Her stomach lurched.\n\nShe gripped the sill and looked out.\n\nThe Rue had not broken yet, but it had changed. Rooflines dipped a little more than they had that morning. The cooper’s shop across the way leaned a little harder. The whole street felt as if someone had shifted a shim under it.\n\nPeople went on walking. A woman adjusted a basket. A carter flicked his reins. A child ran along the cobbles.\n\nNone of them seemed to feel it.\n\nThe ringing climbed further, past where she could track it. The edges of her vision blurred.\n\nHer gaze caught on a small mirror hanging near the window. Her reflection looked back: pale, eyes wide.\n\nA fine line appeared on the glass, stretching from corner to corner. For a moment, the two halves reflected slightly different angles of the room, as if one were a fraction of a second ahead.\n\nHer fingers dug into the sill. The wood pressed into her palm, solid and ordinary.\n\n“Hold,” she whispered—to the wall, the glass, herself.\n\nThe hum filled her chest and spine. It felt like waiting for something to give.\n\nThe river-door frame buzzed against its supports. The lead lines shivered. The empty spaces in the pattern looked less like gaps for glass and more like holes that led nowhere.\n\nHer sense of balance slipped. For a breath, up and down meant nothing. Her body insisted she was falling. The floor under her boots insisted otherwise.\n\nShe did not know where that fall would end. She only knew that when it did, the workshop and the street outside would not return to the shapes they had held that morning.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Three – When the World Was Pierced\n\n**Sixth Casting, Quench of Rising Heat, Crucible Two**  \n*Torrin Forge, Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nJhace worked the bar the way he always did when he needed to think.\n\nLift, strike, turn. Count without meaning to.\n\nForty-seven, forty-eight, forty-nine.\n\nThe iron on the anvil held a solid cherry glow. The outline of the first hinge leaf was there, shoulders broad, spine thick. The bellows sighed with each press of his foot. The forge smelled of coal and old quenches. Outside, the Rue of Saint Merro made its usual noise: wheels on cobble, a hawker calling bread, distant clatter from the cooper’s yard.\n\nHe struck again.\n\nThe hammer’s ring came back wrong.\n\nThe sound itself was clean, but something in the air caught it and stretched it thin. A high, sharp thread hung on after the main note died, too pure to belong to iron or brick.\n\nHe lifted the hammer and listened.\n\nThe thread stayed.\n\nThe nails in the ceiling beam shivered. The surface of the quench trough quivered and formed regular little rings. The hairs on his forearms lifted.\n\n“All right,” he said under his breath. “What are you, then?”\n\nHe crossed to the shutters and shoved them open.\n\nThe Rue had started to go quiet. People stood in the street, heads tilted back. A boy held his cart horse’s reins in both hands; the animal planted its hooves and leaned against the harness like it wanted to climb backward out of the world.\n\nJhace followed their gaze.\n\nThe northern sky wore a scratch.\n\nIt ran from east to west, thin and straight, as if someone had taken a knife to the blue. Along that line, the color looked harder, almost glazed.\n\nA point of light moved along it, low in the north.\n\nAt first it could have been a star in the wrong place. Then it grew. It sharpened into a dot with edges. It lengthened, thickening at the middle, tapering to a sharp tip and a trailing end. It glared white where sun struck glassy surfaces, dulled to pewter and stone along other bands.\n\nWhatever it was, it rode the line. It did not fall in an arc. It came on in a straight track from the north, fast enough that his eyes had trouble believing the change in size from one breath to the next.\n\nThe high tone in the air climbed with it. It felt like a wire drawn tighter with every finger-width the thing advanced.\n\nNo one in the street spoke anymore. Even the horse’s noise dropped to harsh, wet breaths.\n\nThe object dropped lower in the northern sky, then began to cross above them. From where Jhace stood, it seemed to come in at a slant from over the outer hills toward the city’s far side. The central body was thick—thicker than any tower he’d ever seen—tapering to a knife point at the forward end and a shredded, irregular tail behind. The tail looked like it had scraped against stone somewhere else and kept the scars.\n\nGlass, his mind said, because of the hard way it caught the light. Sand fused into long ripples. Bands of metal, dark and bright. Pieces of other things glued into it by speed and pressure.\n\nIt never slowed.\n\nAs it crossed higher overhead, the note in his bones reached a pitch his ears stopped registering as sound. His teeth ached. His vision buzzed at the edges.\n\nThen Te’Oga truly felt it.\n\nThe first shove came up through his feet, a hard, vertical jolt that punched his spine. The high tone snapped. Dust leaped from the floor.\n\nThe next movement wasn’t up and down. It was sideways.\n\nThe whole street rolled from north to south, as if someone had taken the world by its northern pole and given it a violent tug. Buildings leaned, tried to keep their footing, and failed. The forge chimney cracked with a sharp, tearing sound. The anvil hopped. Tools came off their hooks and hit the floor.\n\nJhace grabbed the window frame with both hands as the floor drove his boots toward the southern wall. His stomach dropped in a way that had nothing to do with where his eyes said the ground was.\n\nAnother shove followed the first, from a slightly different angle. The sequence made no sense if you thought about weight sitting on solid stone. It made a terrible kind of sense if something huge had punched into the northern crust, pushed on through, and was still going.\n\nHe didn’t have those words. He had the feeling of it.\n\nThe forge room twisted around him. The old familiar lean of the building—slight, to the south—was wiped out and replaced in a breath. For an instant, up felt closer to the north wall. Then the world fought back, found a new tilt, and held there.\n\nSomewhere across the city, something massive collapsed. The sound followed the motion: a deep, grinding roar, not one crash but many—towers folding, vaults failing, stone meeting stone.\n\nHe heard the bell of Saint Merro begin to toll, not in any pattern. The tower must have lurched hard enough to set it swinging on its own.\n\nThe object in the sky did not stop when the ground moved.\n\nIt crossed overhead, close enough now that he could see texture along its length—frozen waves of fused sand, veins of metal, torn places where it had left pieces behind somewhere he’d never see. Its forward point was already past them, the thick middle directly above, the trailing end dragging scars through the air.\n\nHe had the absurd thought that the city was a knot on a string, and this thing was pulling the string straight through.\n\nAs it moved toward the southern horizon, the main shock gave way to smaller, staggered pulses. Each one came from deeper underfoot, a long, rolling movement from north toward south. The Rue’s cobbles shifted under his boots in steps, like a board ratcheted by an unseen hand.\n\nThe thin line in the sky brightened where the object passed. For a heartbeat, the path behind it glowed like hot metal. Then the light dulled, leaving only a faint scar.\n\nThe thing itself shrank as quickly as it had grown, already far to the south, still on the same straight line. It never arced. It never slowed. It passed over the southern lip of his world and kept going.\n\nThe bell’s wild ringing cut off in mid-swing. The last note ended in a crunch of falling stone.\n\nThe ground kept moving in long, slow rolls. They were smaller than the first shove, but they went on. North to south. North to south. Each one a little weaker, as if whatever had done the pushing was further away now but still not finished with them.\n\nPlaster split along the wall beside his window. A crack raced from ceiling to floor, fast and clean. Dust poured out. Across the alley, the cooper’s front wall bulged and held. The house beyond that lost part of its roof and sent slates sliding into the street.\n\nFine grit started to fall from the air. Some of it was ordinary: plaster, mortar, powdered brick. Some of it caught the light in a hard way and drifted rather than dropped.\n\nThe taste of metal flooded his mouth. Not forge-taste, not blood. A thin, dry tang, like biting a nail.\n\nOne of the later pulses hit then, slower but still strong enough to throw him off balance. The floor rolled under him from north to south and didn’t quite roll back all the way. When it settled, the angle of the room had changed for good.\n\nHis body knew it before his eyes did. His knees, his spine, his inner ear all shouted that “down” had swung a fraction toward the north.\n\nThe Rue shouted with him in its own way.\n\nA house further up tore away from its neighbor. A wall on the lower side dropped in one piece, spilling its contents into the street. Somewhere out of sight, a gas lamp shattered and flared, then died in falling dust.\n\nHe forced his hands off the window frame and backed away.\n\nThe doorway had warped. A straight crack ran above it. He ducked under and took the stairs down, head low.\n\nThe lower shop had shifted half a step on its foundations. His father’s old quench barrel had slid and tipped. Water sloshed out in a rhythm that didn’t match the last tremor. It kept rocking even when the ground stilled, out of time with everything else.\n\nHe shoved the main door and spilled into the street.\n\nThe Rue of Saint Merro listed along its length. The old southward lean that every builder cursed was gone. Now most of the tilt ran north–south. Houses that had rested comfortably crooked for years were abruptly wrong in new directions.\n\nDust sat in the air like a second, thicker atmosphere.\n\nPeople stumbled through it. A woman clutched a child whose eyes were wide and white. A man knelt beside a body half under a fallen signboard. Someone laughed too loudly and didn’t seem to be able to stop.\n\nAnother long roll moved under his boots, weaker but still from north toward south. The world felt like a ball that had been nudged off its stand and was trying to find a new place to rest.\n\nHe looked up again.\n\nThe bright body was small now, a streak against the far southern sky. The line it had come along remained: a faint seam, slightly brighter than the surrounding blue, running from the north behind him over the city and away.\n\nIt never bent.\n\nFine dust continued to fall. The ordinary grit landed and stayed. The other kind sank in.\n\nSomething light and hard tapped the back of his right hand.\n\nHe swatted at it on reflex. His palm met skin. He didn’t see a shard. The feeling didn’t care.\n\nCold slid into the bone beneath his knuckles, followed by a hot, cramped ache. A faint thread of pale light flickered along the vein there, up toward his wrist, and went out. His hand felt briefly too tight for its own skin.\n\nHe flexed his fingers. They moved, but the joints felt like he’d spent a day hammering without rest.\n\nA later aftershock rolled through. It was small, but it came on the same north–south line. He braced his feet and rode it.\n\nThe river’s sound reached him then, though it lay lower down the hill and out of sight. It roared too loud, the way it did in storm floods, but sharper. There was stone in it.\n\nHe could stand in the street and try to map all of it: the line in the sky, the pulses underfoot, the new lean of the Rue.\n\nAcross the alley, Merrow Glass & Mirrors was still standing.\n\nThe sign hung by one chain. A crack ran up from the doorway past the second-floor window. An upper shutter had torn free and lay in the street in splinters. Through the gap, he saw glass moving in broken light.\n\n“Tiffani,” he said.\n\nHis throat was dry; the word scraped.\n\nHe started toward the shop. A beam from a collapsed upper room blocked half the way. He climbed over it. The timber flexed under his weight. For a moment he could feel the strain in its grain as clearly as the strain in his own arm.\n\nHe dropped down on the other side. Dust and glittering flecks puffed up around his boots, then sank into cracks and cloth.\n\nAnother weak, long heave passed under him—from north to south, because that was what the world did now.\n\nHe hit the glass shop’s door with his shoulder. The frame had torqued, but the latch tore free. Inside, the light was wrong and the air tasted of lime and that same thin metal note.\n\n“Tiffani!” he shouted, and ran toward the back stairs while the planet finished, somewhere deep below, the motion of being punched through.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Four – Shards That Listen\n\n**Seventh Casting, Quench of Falling Glass, Crucible Two**  \n*Merrow Glass & Mirrors, Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nThe fall ended in a jolt that snapped Tiffani’s teeth together.\n\nThe workshop stopped stretching. Walls, ceiling, floor snapped back into place, but not exactly as they’d been. The lean had deepened. She felt it in her knees.\n\nThe river-door frame did not accept this new angle. One of its braces gave way. The whole weight of glass and lead dropped.\n\nShe moved without planning. Her hands caught the bottom rail. The frame drove her down a step, feet sliding on grit. Her hips shifted. She bent her knees, spread the load through her legs and spine.\n\nThe door jerked, then settled against her shoulder instead of continuing to fall.\n\nDust poured through the broken roof. Some of it was familiar: plaster, brick, ash. Some of it was not. It glinted as it fell and sank into whatever it touched.\n\nCold pinpricks dotted her left hand, her jaw, the hollow at her throat. The sensation lasted only a breath, then vanished under her skin. A tight, bright feeling ran along her bones.\n\nHer sense of the room changed.\n\nShe could tell which beam above her was carrying too much. The floorboards behind her right heel were close to splitting. The wall the frame leaned against was taking more load than its neighbor.\n\nThe information did not come from sight. It was a quiet pressure under her skin.\n\nThe frame shook, trying to twist. She shifted her grip, sliding her hand along the rail until the strain in her shoulders lessened. The pattern in her bones agreed it was better.\n\nMore glass broke.\n\nSheets crashed from the racks. Shards spun through the air. One curved piece came toward her face, edge first.\n\nShe turned her head and said, “No.”\n\nThe shard veered. It struck the frame and fell. The sound it made when it hit matched the thin ringing in her ears.\n\nHer heart pounded. Sweat stung the cut on her forearm she hadn’t noticed making.\n\nThis is wrong, she thought. But the thing under her hands was very real, very heavy.\n\nThe floor rolled again, slower this time. Something deep in the building failed with a crack. The entire structure shifted and then rested in a new position.\n\nThe sharp sound in the air loosened into a steadier hum.\n\nTiffani eased the river-door back into the damaged braces. Her arms shook. When she stepped away, it stayed up, crooked but upright.\n\nShe walked across a floor now layered in broken glass and dust. Small sparks of sensation jumped up through her boots wherever she stepped, as if tiny currents were moving between her and the shards.\n\nThe rack near the furnace had collapsed. The window had lost most of its panes. A section of ceiling near the stair had fallen away, exposing beams and sky.\n\nOn the workbench lay something that had not been there before.\n\nIt was the size of her fist. Some surfaces were as smooth as poured glass. Others were rough and pitted. Veins of dull metal ran through it. Its color sat somewhere between stone and cooled slag.\n\nWhen she looked straight at it, she thought she saw a faint change—like a slow pulse—running through the interior.\n\nFine glitter still drifted down. Some of it landed on her arms and face. Some caught the bench and the fallen beams. The rest disappeared into cracks and into the open mouths of people outside who did not yet know they were breathing it in.\n\nAnother grain struck the corner of her jaw. Cool, then gone. The muscles there clenched and eased.\n\nSounds sharpened.\n\nShe could pick out the creak of a damaged beam three houses over, the smash of a window on the next street, the uneven rhythm of running feet downstairs. The city was loud in a way it had never been.\n\nShe put a hand to her ear, then lowered it. Touch did nothing.\n\nShe listened.\n\nScreams. Names. A heavier noise as some large structure gave way and stopped. Under it all, a constant rushing from the direction of the river, louder than any tide she remembered.\n\nThe floor felt different under her boots. The old lean had changed. She shifted her stance until her weight distributed in a way that did not hurt.\n\nFine, she thought. If the building chooses a new balance, so will I.\n\nShe stepped toward the stairs. Every shard in her path seemed to lean toward her attention.\n\n“Stay,” she said quietly. “You’ve done enough.”\n\nNone moved.\n\nShe reached the stairhead and gripped the rail. It creaked but held.\n\nThrough the broken boards and gaps below, she heard the front room and the street beyond full of movement and noise.\n\nA heavy impact hit the shop door. Wood cracked. Hinges protested.\n\n“Tiffani!”\n\nThe voice came up through the structure as much as through the air.\n\nShe stopped.\n\nDust swirled in the stairwell. Her left hand tingled. The lump on the bench behind her brightened once, then dimmed.\n\nAnother blow on the door. Something splintered. Light slipped through a new crack in the floor below.\n\n“Tiffani!”\n\nJhace.\n\nShe stood there, caught between the workshop and the voice below, with the new hum in her bones and the city shifting around them. For a few heartbeats, everything waited to see which way she would move.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Five – Aftershocks in the Rue\n\n**Ninth Casting, Anneal of Cooling Ash, Crucible Two**  \n*Merrow Glass & Mirrors, Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nJhace hit the shop door with his shoulder. The latch tore out of the frame. He stumbled inside.\n\nThe showroom was a wreck. Shelves had fallen. Glass lay piled ankle-deep in places, ground fine where heavy things had landed on it. A mirror face-down on the floor gave back a broken reflection from a spiderweb of cracks.\n\n“Tiffani!” His voice bounced off tilted walls. The echo came back slightly warped.\n\nHe picked his way through the mess. Shards shifted under his boots. Some seemed to cling a fraction longer to his soles than they should.\n\nThe stair to the workshop leaned more than the forge’s had. The handrail hummed against his palm with a faint vibration that matched the line along his veins.\n\nHe climbed fast.\n\nThe workshop door stood half open. He pushed it wider.\n\nTiffani stood in the center of the room, dust in her hair, blood drying along one side of her face. The river-door frame rested against the wall behind her, not in pieces on the floor.\n\n“You’re alive,” he said.\n\n“So are you,” she answered.\n\nHer voice was steady. Her eyes were hard and too bright. The veins in her left hand showed faintly pale beneath the skin.\n\nHe moved closer. His own hand still tingled from where the shard had gone in. The line from the back of his hand toward his wrist was faint but there.\n\nHer gaze flicked to it. “You have it too.”\n\n“Something hit me,” he said. “Went in. Didn’t come back out.”\n\n“Same,” she said.\n\nFor a moment they stood there, breathing in sync with the furnace’s slow draw.\n\nA shout came from below. “Mistress Merrow!”\n\n“Marc,” she said. The first crack in her voice.\n\nThey both turned for the stairs.\n\nThe lower room was worse. A beam had fallen at an angle. Marc lay half under it, glass piled around his legs. His face was streaked with dust and tears. He was breathing fast.\n\n“I tried to get out,” he said as soon as he saw them. “The floor—”\n\n“Save your breath,” Jhace said.\n\nHe set his hands on the beam. It should have been beyond him. The wood was thick. Plaster and brick still clung to it.\n\nHe pulled.\n\nThe humming in his bones rose. The faint ache in his hand sharpened. The beam resisted, then moved. Not much—just enough.\n\n“Now,” he said.\n\nTiffani slid in and hauled Marc free by the shoulders. Glass ground under his heel. He yelped as weight came off trapped muscles.\n\nJhace eased the beam down. His arms shook. His heart hammered.\n\nMarc clung to Tiffani for a moment, then pulled back, embarrassed even now. “I’m fine,” he lied. “Just twisted.”\n\nHis right leg did not want to take full weight. He leaned against the least damaged wall and tried to straighten.\n\n“Sit,” Tiffani said. No room in her tone for argument.\n\nHe sat.\n\nThey checked quickly for worse injuries. No deep cuts. No bones at obvious angles. A swelling forming along his shin where the beam had pressed.\n\n“The furnace?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Upstairs,” Tiffani said. “Still burning.”\n\n“And the street?” Marc asked.\n\nThey went to the front window.\n\nThe glass was cracked, but some pieces still held. Tiffani rested her fingers against one. The pane vibrated under her touch.\n\nOutside, the Rue showed the cost of the last few minutes. Houses leaned against each other. Some had lost their fronts. Dust hung in the air, lit from above by the same thin line he had seen from the forge.\n\n“There,” Marc said, following his gaze. “What is that?”\n\nThe seam crossed the sky, faint but clear.\n\n“I don’t know,” Jhace said. It was the only honest answer.\n\n“Whatever it is,” Tiffani said, “it’s still doing something. Listen.”\n\nHe focused past the noise of people and rubble.\n\nHe heard the river. It was moving faster through channels that no longer matched their old beds. Stone under the street shifted in small, grinding movements.\n\n“It feels like it hasn’t finished settling,” he said.\n\n“Because it hasn’t,” she replied.\n\nMarc swallowed. “The Guild will come. The Church. They’ll ask how this place stayed standing when others didn’t.”\n\n“They’ll ask in whose favor it happened,” Tiffani said. “And whether they can repeat it.”\n\nJhace looked at his hand, at the faint line that hadn’t been there an hour ago. Then at hers.\n\n“First we make sure nothing else in here falls on our heads,” he said. “Then we look at the furnace. Then we see what the river-door looks like now.”\n\n“And after that?” she asked.\n\n“After that,” he said, “we decide which part of this we admit to knowing.”\n\nMarc gave a short, nervous laugh. “So we lie to the Guild.”\n\n“We choose our words,” Tiffani corrected. “Very carefully.”\n\nJhace turned from the window. The seam in the sky stayed where it was, a mark that promised this was not over. In the room with him were a cracked furnace, a tilted wall, a half-finished door—and two people whose hands now carried lines he did not understand.\n\nHe picked up a broom that had fallen and started clearing glass from around Marc’s feet. It was as good a first step as any.\n\n---\n\n## Interlude I – Mobel at the Edge of the Sea\n\n**Sixth Casting, Quench of Wrong Water, Crucible Two**  \n*Silverport Harbor, Underface district*\n\nMobel checked the tide gauge at the end of the western pier. The marker showed three finger-widths below mean, but the water was still receding. It had been receding for two hours. The normal ebb took six hours to drop that far; this had done it in four.\n\nThe harbor master had sent a runner to the Guild hall. The boy had come back alone. The factor was in a meeting. The factor was always in a meeting.\n\nMobel walked back along the pier, boots leaving prints on timbers that were usually below the waterline. The *Sea Hawk* sat on mud now, hull canted to starboard, keel exposed. The *Harrow* was worse—stranded on the harbor bottom, rudder buried in silt. Mooring chains that should have been taut lay slack, coiled on the exposed seabed.\n\nToo much water gone, too fast.\n\nHe stopped halfway down the pier and looked toward the harbor mouth. The sea outside the breakwater had dropped, but the line where water met air ran lower than his memory of any Spring Tide. Rocks he’d never seen were bare.\n\nGulls circled, restless. They knew the pattern of tides. This wasn’t it.\n\nThe ringing started as he reached the dockmaster’s shed. High, thin, threading through the usual harbor sounds: rope creak, gull cry, wave slap. It reminded him of a stay pulled too tight, just before it sang itself apart.\n\nHe stepped inside the shed. The window looked straight out at the harbor mouth. The sky beyond was a flat blue, no clouds.\n\nA line cut through it, horizon to horizon. Hair-thin, faintly bright.\n\nHe wiped the glass with his sleeve. The line stayed.\n\nThe water in the drinking bucket at his elbow rippled. No wind touched its surface.\n\nThe harbor bell began to ring. Not the slow, measured toll for time. A fast, wrong clamor.\n\nMobel ran back out.\n\nThe tide was returning. Not as a gentle rise. Not even as a usual storm surge. It came in steps, each one harder than the last.\n\nThe first surge hit the outer pilings, climbed up their weed-dark sides, and lifted the *Sea Hawk* two hands in less than a breath. The second came on its heels and slammed the freed hull into the pier. Planks jumped under Mobel’s boots.\n\n“Cut her loose!” someone shouted. Too late. The third surge hit with wreckage already in its teeth: broken posts, half a lighter, someone’s small fishing skiff torn to boards.\n\nThe water’s surface went glass-slick between waves. When he looked down, he could see the sky’s new line reflected. Along it, something moved.\n\nAt first it was just a point. Then it elongated, sharpening and thickening at once. A long body, swelling at the middle, tapering to points at both ends. Parts of it caught the sun the way glass did. Other sections showed dull bands, like metal and stone fused.\n\nIt didn’t fall in an arc. It came in straight, riding that line, and the sea below it answered wrong.\n\nThe ground shuddered.\n\nNot just the pier. The pilings. The stone under them. The city itself. The shock ran up his legs and into his teeth.\n\nThe dockmaster’s shed slid sideways. One wall gave up and dropped into the water. Mobel saw the dockmaster’s cap whirl once in the foam and vanish.\n\nHe didn’t go after it. There was nowhere safe to go after anything.\n\nA surge higher than the rest rolled through the harbor mouth. It tore the end off the breakwater as if it were made of stacked toys and brought the pieces with it. Piers snapped in sections. Ships rose, broke free, and turned sideways under the force. Two collided mid-channel. The sound of their hulls coming apart was like a tree splitting.\n\nMobel ran.\n\nThe western pier heaved under him. Boards tore loose. He jumped the last three and hit the harbor steps. They were usually slimed. Now they were just wet stone.\n\nThe shard hit his leg halfway up.\n\nHe didn’t see it coming. Just a hard impact on the left shin, enough to stagger him. Pain flared, hot and clear. He grabbed for the banister, missed, and dropped to one knee.\n\nThe next surge reached the bottom of the steps and climbed. Cold flooded over his boots. For a moment he thought it would take him, drag him back. The water rose to his calves, his knees, then stopped. Not because it had spent itself against the stone. Because something in his leg said *here and no higher*.\n\nHe could feel it: a tension in the bone just below the shard, like a line tied to something far offshore.\n\nMobel hauled himself up the remaining steps before the next surge arrived.\n\nWhen he reached the top and looked back, the harbor he’d worked for fifteen years was gone. Not empty—full of motion and wreckage—but gone in any shape he recognized. Piers torn loose, riding the water like rafts. Ships on their sides. The breakwater a jagged line of white where waves broke against fresh stumps of stone.\n\nHis shin throbbed. He tore his trouser leg open.\n\nThe shard had sunk through skin and muscle and lodged against the tibia. It was the size of a walnut, glassy in places, rough in others. Pale veins ran through it, brighter than they ought to be.\n\nEach time a new surge built outside the harbor, he felt the pressure increase there first. The next wave of water up the channel came after.\n\nHe wrapped the leg in a strip torn from his shirt and stood. His balance had changed. The ground seemed to tilt in a way that had nothing to do with the aftershocks and everything to do with the sea.\n\n“Mobel!” someone shouted from further up the street. “This way!”\n\nHe limped toward the voice. The city above the docks was chaos: people running, buildings cracked, a cart upended with its wheels still spinning. All of it overlaid with the pull in his shinbone, the push of the next surge already on its way.\n\nHe did what harbor pilots did. He read water.\n\n“Lower city floods in an hour!” he yelled at the nearest knot of people. “Get uphill. Leave the carts. Take the children.”\n\nThey stared at him, then at the state of him, then at the harbor. Another piece of pier went past in the swell.\n\nSome ran. Some didn’t.\n\nThe shard in his leg warmed, a steady, unwelcome anchor.\n\nHe turned inland, toward the roads that led away from Silverport and toward the Crown. Toward whoever needed to be told that the tides no longer answered to charts.\n\nThe sea would keep coming whether anyone listened or not. But he could feel where it would go, and when. That was his curse now.\n\nHe limped toward the city, carrying the ocean in his bone.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Six – Strange Resonances\n\n**Tenth Casting, Quench of Quiet Air, Crucible Two**  \n*Merrow Glass & Mirrors, Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nThe furnace was the only steady thing left.\n\nTiffani kept it banked low, coals at a deep red. Enough light to work. Not enough heat to crack what glass remained.\n\nMost of the workshop was wreckage. One lead pot had split. Another had hairline cracks along the rim. A third sat solid, blackened but holding. Three full sheets of crown glass had survived where they’d been wrapped in burlap behind the furnace. Everything else lay in drifts of shards.\n\nShe picked through the piles with careful hands. Anything bigger than her palm went on the salvage table. The rest she swept into buckets for cullet. When her fingers brushed certain pieces, they tingled, like touching metal on a winter day.\n\nShe reached toward a triangular shard on the bench, no larger than her hand. Before she touched it, it twitched. Only a finger’s width, but enough to scrape a faint line in the soot.\n\nShe froze.\n\n“Move,” she said quietly.\n\nThe shard slid across the oak. A slow, steady glide. It stopped when she stopped wanting it to move.\n\nHer left hand burned along the knuckles. Blue-silver shadow sat under the skin now, just visible when the furnace light caught it. She flexed her fingers. The joints clicked.\n\nDownstairs, Marc shifted on his bed of drop cloths. The boards carried the movement up to her feet. He’d fallen asleep mid-sentence an hour ago, exhaustion finally winning. His breathing below was slow and uneven.\n\nShe turned to the cracked mirror leaning against the wall. The break was clean, corner to corner. Each half held to the backing by habit more than sense.\n\nTiffani set her left palm flat on the glass.\n\nA hum climbed into her arm, the same pitch that lived behind her eyes since the dust fell. She felt along the crack without seeing it: edges pulling apart, tiny chips wanting to fall free.\n\n“Together,” she said under her breath.\n\nThe two sides pressed inward. The gap narrowed. The crack line softened. Under her palm, the glass shifted from two pieces to one piece with a scar. When she lifted her hand, the mirror held. The line was still visible, duller than before.\n\nHer palm went numb to the wrist. Pins-and-needles ran up her forearm, sharp enough to make her grit her teeth. She leaned against the workbench and counted breaths until she could feel her fingers again.\n\nTwenty-seven.\n\nShe walked to the river-door frame. It leaned where she had propped it, against a back wall that now carried more weight than it was built for. She pressed her ear to the lead. The building’s strain came through as sound and pressure both: a low grinding under her feet, a high whine in the wall, one timber upstairs taking more than its share.\n\nThat beam would go if no one helped it.\n\nShe closed her eyes and followed the strain, mapping it in her mind: where it entered from the roof, where it crossed the beam, where it sank into the far wall. She nudged it sideways, the way she might shift weight from one leg to the other while carrying a heavy pane.\n\nThe whine dropped a shade. The grinding eased. The beam held.\n\nHer knuckles burned again. The patina along them darkened, and for a moment her elbow felt full of hot needles. She counted breaths: ten, eleven, twelve. The worst of it faded, but the stiffness stayed.\n\n“Mistress?”\n\nMarc’s voice came from the stair. He stood in the doorway, hair sticking up, eyes over-bright.\n\n“You should be asleep,” she said.\n\n“You fixed the mirror.” He pointed. “It was cracked. Now it isn’t.”\n\n“I pushed the pieces together,” she said. “While the mortar was still loose.”\n\n“The shards moved,” he said. “On the bench. You told them to. I saw.”\n\nShe wiped dust from her palms onto her trousers and faced him fully. “You saw too much today. You’re tired. Your mind is filling in gaps.”\n\nHe stayed where he was. “Your hands are glowing.”\n\nShe looked down. The blue-silver under her skin wasn’t light, not really, but it caught the furnace glow in a way normal veins didn’t.\n\n“It’s in you,” Marc said. “Like the dust.”\n\n“It’s in everything,” she answered. “The bricks. The iron. The river. The air.” She stepped closer. “Listen to me, Marc. If the Guild hears you say ‘glowing hands,’ they will take this shop. They will take me. They might take you.”\n\n“Why me?”\n\n“Because you saw.” She let him see how serious she was. “Because you could testify.”\n\nHe swallowed. “What is it? Magic?”\n\n“I don’t know what it is,” she said. “I know what it does. It lets me feel where things are about to break and push back, a little. It lets glass hear me when I speak. It takes something from my bones every time I ask.”\n\n“That sounds like magic.”\n\n“That sounds like work with a cost.” She rubbed her left hand. The numbness had shrunk back to her fingertips. “Same as standing too long at the furnace, or lifting glass wrong. Only faster.”\n\nHe looked at the mirror again. “You’re going to tell the Guild?”\n\n“I’m going to tell them the building is damaged and I’m doing my best to keep it standing,” she said. “Nothing more.”\n\n“They’ll know,” Marc said. “They always know.”\n\n“Then we give them less to know.” She picked up a smaller shard from the bench and held it out. “See this?”\n\nHe nodded.\n\nShe set it on the wood. “Watch.”\n\nShe thought *stay*. The shard did not move. She lifted her hand, stepped back.\n\n“Now you,” she said.\n\nMarc frowned, looked at the shard, and whispered, “Move.”\n\nNothing happened.\n\n“Again,” she said.\n\nHe tried twice more. No twitch. No hum she could feel.\n\n“It listens to you,” he said. “Not me.”\n\n“It listens to anyone carrying enough of that dust inside their bones.” She nodded toward his chest. “You breathed some. We all did. It hasn’t…caught yet, in you.”\n\n“Will it?” His voice was very small.\n\n“I don’t know,” she said, and it was the truth. “Go lie down. We start counting what we can save at first light. The Guild factor will come. We need our numbers clear.”\n\nHe lingered a moment longer, then went down. The boards told her when he lay back on the cloth, when he shifted, when his breathing finally settled.\n\nTiffani sat with her back against the brick, hands resting on her knees, and listened to the workshop.\n\nFurnace, steady. Roof, strained. Second-floor beam, holding for now. Glass on the bench, quiet unless she looked at it too long.\n\nOutside, the Rue creaked and groaned. Somewhere lower in the city, the river hit a new turn in its bed and complained about it.\n\nShe flexed her fingers. The patina along her knuckles caught the red light. With each small use, the dust inside her settled deeper. She could feel that too.\n\nShe counted breaths until the pain eased back to something she could ignore, and waited for morning.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Seven – The Guild’s Net\n\n**First Casting, Anneal of Hot Ash, Crucible Three**  \n*Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nBy midmorning the Rue sounded like a repair yard. Hammers, shouted orders, the dull thuds of stone pried loose and dropped. Smoke hung between leaning walls.\n\nJhace worked at a fallen roof. A main beam had come down across a doorway, pinning it shut. Nails held where they should have given way.\n\nHe set the claw of his hammer and gripped the first nail with his tongs.\n\nThe iron hummed against his hand. He tugged. The nail came out straighter than it went in. The crushed bend uncurled as if it had never taken a load.\n\nThat wasn’t how metal behaved. Not before.\n\nHe set the nail aside. The next did the same.\n\n“You made it unbent,” a small voice said.\n\nHe looked up. A girl sat on the rubble pile, knees drawn to her chest, dust in her hair. Eight, maybe nine.\n\n“Nail was tired of being crooked,” he said.\n\n“Your hammer sings,” she said. “My ears hear it.”\n\nHe listened. Under the clang and scrape of the street, he could pick it out now: a low tone hanging under every strike, matching the faint ache in his hand.\n\nHe pulled two more nails. Straightened wood. Lifted the beam enough for two neighbors to wedge bricks and pry bars under it. They’d get the door open without him.\n\nA runner in Guild colors found him there. Young, breathless, a strip of vermillion cloth tied to his sleeve.\n\n“Torrin?” the runner asked.\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Factor wants you at the south end. Now.”\n\nThe south end of Saint Merro met the road to the harbor. The surge had reached here as a churning wall of water and wreckage. The lower houses were simply gone. The street ended in a jumble of stone and splintered beams.\n\nA man sat on one of the blocks, his trouser leg slit to show a bandage. The bandage was already red in a spreading patch. A shard shone through the cloth, wedged deep against the bone. It pulsed, faint and steady.\n\n“Jhace Torrin?” the man said. “You’re the hinge-maker.”\n\n“Was,” Jhace said. “Forge isn’t there anymore.”\n\n“Mobel,” the man said. “Harbor pilot.”\n\nHe tapped his shin. “This piece tells me what the water is about to do.”\n\nJhace crouched to look. The shard had fused to the tibia. Blue-pale threads ran from it up into the muscle.\n\n“How?” Jhace asked.\n\n“I feel pressure here.” Mobel tapped above the shard. “Builds before the water moves. That last surge? Knew it was coming half an hour before anyone saw it. Felt like someone pushing on the inside of my leg.”\n\nJhace believed him. The shard in his own hand had begun to warm each time the ground rolled before an aftershock.\n\nA woman in a vermillion-striped coat stood nearby, watching them both. Clipboard tucked under one arm. Corlexi, Guild factor. He’d seen her marking buildings all morning, chalking symbols on doors: red for unsafe, yellow for repairable, white for inspected.\n\n“Two of you now,” she said. “Good. The patterns hold.”\n\n“What patterns?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Shard in the leg for pilots. Shard in the hand for smiths. Shard in the bones for glassworkers.” Her gaze flicked toward the Rue, toward Tiffani’s shop. “The dust doesn’t pick at random.”\n\nShe stepped closer. “Show me your hands, Torrin.”\n\nHe hesitated, then held them out. The faint pale line from the back of his right hand toward his wrist was clearer today. It branched at the joint, following a vein. His fingers shook a little, even at rest.\n\nCorlexi measured the tremor with her eyes. “And the shard?”\n\nHe pulled the sliver from his pocket. It lay on his palm like a dull piece of slag, but it was warm. When he focused on it, his fingers tingled.\n\nShe opened a leather pouch. “Guild pays for recovered pieces. Three scrip for inert. More for responsive.”\n\n“It’s not for sale,” he said.\n\n“Everything is for sale,” she said. “Until it isn’t.” She closed the pouch. “You’ve been straightening nails no one else can pull. We’ve seen it.”\n\n“The hammer helps,” he said.\n\n“The hammer resonates,” she corrected. “We’re cataloguing that. People like you are to report to the Guild hall. There’s talk of protective contracts. Work, board, study.”\n\n“Contracts that let me walk back out?” he asked.\n\nShe didn’t answer directly. “Your forge is rubble. You’re standing in a street held together by luck and glass. The Guild offers structure.”\n\n“With a lock on it,” Jhace said.\n\nCorlexi turned to the runner. “Salee will take your statement later,” she told Jhace. “Inventory of tools. Any unusual materials. For now, keep your hammer on jobs that matter.”\n\nShe walked off toward another fallen wall, calling for measurements.\n\nA different runner—shorter, with close-cropped hair and a slate under her arm—stayed. She gave Jhace a level look.\n\n“Salee,” she said by way of name. “She won’t ask twice, you know.”\n\n“She can ask again anyway,” Jhace said.\n\nSalee smirked, just a little. “Protective contracts aren’t about protecting you. They’re about protecting everyone else from what you can do. That’s the word in the Guild hall.”\n\n“And what’s the word in the street?” he asked.\n\n“That the Church is making its own lists,” she said. “And the Crown doesn’t like being last.”\n\nMobel grunted. “They already took my partner,” he said. “Pilot named Lisse. She could feel when the tide wanted to change early. Took her to the Glasshouses in Silverport. Last time I saw her, she didn’t know me.”\n\nSalee’s jaw tightened. “You didn’t hear that from me.”\n\nJhace closed his hand around the shard. It pulsed against his palm.\n\n“I’ve got work here,” he said. “Beams to lift, nails to pull. Tell your factor my tools stay with me. And so do I.”\n\n“She’ll remember that,” Salee said. “She remembers everything.”\n\nShe moved off to question a stonecutter whose hands shook worse than Jhace’s.\n\nJhace left Mobel with a bucket of clean water and a promise to check back before full Casting. The shard in his pocket warmed as he walked. Each time his hammer met iron, the metal hummed back, as if listening for instructions.\n\nThe Guild had its net out. So did the Church. He could feel both tightening, link by link.\n\nFor the moment, he still walked where he chose.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Eight – First Deliberate Magic\n\n**Third Casting, Quench of Shaking Ground, Crucible Three**  \n*Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nThe next aftershock came without the high warning note. The ground just rolled.\n\nTiffani was on the second floor of a neighboring house, checking a cracked lintel. The building had lost part of its roof, but families still lived on the lower levels. They had nowhere else to go.\n\nThe floor under her boots shifted sideways. She caught the wall with her hand and felt the whole structure lean another finger-width.\n\n“Out!” someone shouted from below. “Everyone out!”\n\nA child screamed. Another voice swore.\n\nAcross a narrow gap of air, the adjoining house’s front wall slumped. A wide transom window over its cellar door bowed inward. The glass pane was big—two arm-lengths by one—set in iron and lead. As the wall leaned, the transom took the load. Cracks spidered from its corners.\n\nIf it went, the bricks above would drop straight onto the cellar stairs.\n\nShe heard the family before she saw them. A man, a woman, at least two children, scrambling in the half-dark space under the transom. The door jammed halfway open, frame racked by the shift.\n\nTiffani ran.\n\nThe stair rattled under her as she took it three at a time. The shop opposite Merrow Glass had a narrow back corridor that led to the shared cellar. She ducked through, shoulders brushing both walls, and came up against the blocked door.\n\nThe transom loomed above it, bowed and shaking. Dust sifted down from the joints. The cracks widened.\n\n“Back against the far wall!” she shouted through the half-open door. “Now!”\n\nThe family scrambled out of sight. She reached up and pressed her left palm flat against the center of the glass. It was cold. It hummed.\n\nEvery weak point in the pane lit up inside her: the places where cooling had gone uneven, the spots where the lead had been too thin, the strains from years of seasonal swelling.\n\n“Hold,” she said.\n\nThe glass resisted. It wanted to follow the wall’s movement, not defy it.\n\n“Hold,” she said again, and meant it with her whole body. Her knees bent. Her spine lined up under her shoulder. She pushed, not with weight but with intent.\n\nThe bow eased. The cracks stopped crawling. The lead groaned, then set. Above, bricks settled closer together instead of falling.\n\nInside, the family’s noise dropped from panicked shouts to muffled sobbing.\n\nHer hand went dead to the elbow. Pain bit into her shoulder, sharp and immediate, as if she’d lifted a full sheet of glass wrong.\n\nThe aftershock rolled on. The building across the alley lost a chimney. Slates crashed into the street in a shower. But the transom held.\n\nA man’s face appeared in the crack of the cellar door. Dust streaked his beard. “What did you just do?”\n\n“Bought you time,” she said, pulling her hand away. The glass stayed put. Lines still marred it, but they were closed lines, not open.\n\nHer arm felt heavy, like it belonged to someone else. She rotated her wrist slowly until the pins-and-needles broke into more manageable aches. She counted the breaths until she could curl her fingers into a fist.\n\nThirty-four.\n\nThe man pushed the door wider. Two children clung to his legs. A woman crouched behind them, clutching a bundle.\n\n“You made the glass…listen,” he said. “I felt it. The wall set back.”\n\n“The wall found a new balance,” she said. “I nudged.”\n\nHe looked at her left hand. The blue-silver under the skin had crept further toward her wrist. “The Church says people like you are a trial,” he said. “The Guild says you’re property.”\n\n“The Church and the Guild can fight over adjectives,” she said. “Get your family out from under that wall. It’s safer in the street than in there.”\n\nHe hesitated. “What should I tell them you did?”\n\n“Tell them your transom held,” she said. “Glassworkers hate to hear about failures.”\n\nHe huffed a short, shaken laugh and herded his family past her into the corridor. They approached the light like people coming out of a deep tunnel.\n\nMarc waited near the front door, bucket in hand. His eyes were too wide.\n\n“That was you,” he said when she stepped into the street. “I heard the glass change.”\n\n“You heard the wall,” she said. Sweat cooled on the back of her neck. Her legs felt unsteady. “Help them find a clear spot away from falling brick. Then go check on the furnace.”\n\n“Your hand.” He grabbed her right wrist, careful not to touch the left. “It’s—”\n\nThe patina had spread. What had been faint at her knuckles was now visible up to the base of her thumb. A thin line ran along the underside of her forearm like a shadow of a vein.\n\n“It’s fine,” she lied.\n\n“You can’t keep doing this,” he said, dropping his voice. “Every time you…push like that, it gets worse. I see it.”\n\n“Every time I don’t, something breaks that didn’t need to,” she said. “Pick which you prefer.”\n\nA man in grey robes with a wheel-and-flame stitched on the sleeve stood at the end of the Rue, watching. He made marks on a slate without looking down.\n\nMarc followed her gaze. “Church,” he said.\n\n“Let him write,” she said. “Ink doesn’t hold walls up.”\n\nThey walked back toward Merrow Glass. Her knees twinged on the slope. The stiffness had spread there too.\n\nInside the workshop, the core-lump sat on the bench where it had landed the day of the Piercing. It pulsed in a slow, even beat. Every time it brightened under the surface, she felt a faint answering throb in her bones.\n\nShe set her good hand on the workbench, caught her breath, and started counting glass again. There were only so many pieces she could hold together with her own body. She needed to know what the Rue could stand without her.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Nine – The Others\n\n**Fifth Casting, Anneal of Falling Nails, Crucible Three**  \n*Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nRain finally came on the third day. Not much. Enough to turn the dust to paste and make the broken stones slick.\n\nJhace worked under it anyway. A neighbor’s workshop roof had dropped three fingers overnight. He and two others wedged it up with timber struts while the owner hauled out anything worth saving.\n\nEach time his hammer met a bent nail or twisted strap, the metal straightened easier than it should. The shard in his pocket warmed in answer. His right hand shook more now, a constant tremor at the edge of usefulness.\n\n“Your hammer’s humming again,” a familiar small voice said.\n\nThe same girl from before stood in the doorway, damp hair plastered to her forehead. “I can’t hear the rain right when it does that,” she added. “Like the sound gets pushed aside.”\n\n“Then I’ll hum less,” he said. “Go back inside. Roof’s not safe.”\n\nShe stuck her tongue out but obeyed.\n\nA runner found him as he set the last brace. Not Salee this time; a boy with a Church badge at his collar and worry lines too deep for his age. “Torrin,” he said. “Come to the southern cordon. Factor says now.”\n\nThe southern cordon had hardened in the last day: posts, chain, and a line of guards in mixed Guild and Crown colors. Beyond them, the road opened toward the lower city and the broken view of the harbor.\n\nMobel leaned against a half-toppled marker stone near the barrier. His bandage was cleaner but thinner. The shard in his shin showed through like a buried ember.\n\n“Surge in two hours,” he said without preamble. “Smaller than the last. Enough to make trouble at the river locks.”\n\n“How do you know?” Jhace asked.\n\n“The same way you know when iron wants to go straight.” Mobel tapped his leg. “It builds here. Then the water answers.”\n\nSalee stood nearby, slate in hand. Beside her was Corlexi, that same clipboard clamped under her arm. Around them, a loose ring of shard-touched: the stonecutter whose hands shook, a cooper, a woman in healer’s green with dark lines under both eyes.\n\n“All right,” Corlexi said. “We’re done pretending this is scattered.” She looked from one to the next. “You feel cracks before they open.” She nodded at the stonecutter. “You never lose water from a barrel.” The cooper. “You close wounds without fire.” The healer. “You read the tide.” Mobel. “You straighten iron that should have stayed bent.” Jhace.\n\nHer eyes tracked the faint markings on their skin: vein-shadows, patina, too-steady or too-shaky hands.\n\n“You’re the start of a list,” she said. “The Church has theirs. The Crown has theirs. The Guild will have ours.”\n\n“Lists for what?” Jhace asked.\n\n“To decide who gets taken,” Mobel muttered.\n\nSalee’s jaw tightened, but she didn’t disagree.\n\n“The Glasshouses in the Upper Crown have empty wings,” Corlexi said. “We’ve seen the requisitions. ‘For study.’” Her tone made it clear what she thought of that word. “The first wave will go voluntary, if they can be convinced. The second wave will not be asked.”\n\n“Where do we stand?” Jhace asked. “First or second?”\n\n“Third,” Corlexi said. “For now. You’re still useful where you are.”\n\nA bell farther down the hill rang unevenly. Someone testing a cracked tower. The rain made its own thin music on the chains of the cordon.\n\nMobel shifted his weight. “There are others,” he said. “Not in the city. West of Silverport. A place they’re starting to call the Cadence. People like us. No lists there yet. Just work and not being locked up.”\n\n“You’ve been?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Once,” Mobel said. “Before the Piercing. Met a woman who could make a bridge plank hold even with three split boards. She listened to the grain.” He shrugged. “Word is the Cadence is growing. People heading that way from the coast. Maybe from inland, too.”\n\nSalee glanced at Corlexi. “If they leave, they’re off the Guild’s books,” she said. “Off the Church’s too.”\n\n“If they stay, they’re in a cage,” Mobel answered.\n\nCorlexi didn’t flinch at that. “No one’s leaving the Rue until the cordon lifts,” she said. “That’s Crown law. For quarantine. For safety.”\n\n“For sorting,” Mobel said.\n\nJhace looked back up the street. From here he could see the crooked line of Saint Merro: his collapsed forge, the intact front of Merrow Glass, the fresh chalk marks on doors and stones.\n\nHe could also see the man in grey Church robes halfway up the slope, watching everything with his wheel-and-flame slate.\n\n“How long until that next surge?” he asked Mobel.\n\n“Two hours,” Mobel said again. “Give or take ten breaths.”\n\n“Then we use the time,” Jhace said.\n\n“For what?” Salee asked.\n\n“To decide who we are before someone else writes it down,” he said.\n\nHe walked back toward the Rue, the shard in his pocket warm against his thigh, the chain at his back humming in the rain.\n\n---\n\n## Chapter Ten – Siege of Saint Merro\n\n**Sixth Casting, Quench of Falling Glass, Crucible Three**  \n*Rue of Saint Merro*\n\nBy dusk, the Rue was an island.\n\nIron posts marked the ends of the street. Chains ran between them, links thick as thumbs. Guards stood at each barrier, spears grounded, cloaks dark with rain. Guild badges, Crown colors, and the occasional Church wheel all mixed in their ranks.\n\nTiffani watched from the upper window. The core-lump on her bench pulsed in a slow, steady rhythm. Each brightening tugged at the nerves in her left hand.\n\n“They’ll come,” Marc said quietly behind her. “For that.” He nodded at the lump. “For you.”\n\n“I know,” she said.\n\n“What are we going to do?”\n\n“Not wait for them in the doorway.” She turned from the window. “Pack what you can carry. Clothes. Food. No glass. No lead. Nothing that shines more than it should.”\n\nHe blinked. “We’re leaving? We can’t. The cordon—”\n\n“Is iron,” she said. “Iron has weak points, same as glass. I can feel them.”\n\nA knock sounded at the street door. Firm, official, not panicked.\n\nJhace’s voice came through a heartbeat later. “It’s me.”\n\nMarc ran down to unlatch it. Jhace stepped in, hair wet, clothes streaked with muck. He carried an oilcloth bundle under one arm.\n\n“It’s finished,” he said as he reached the workshop. He unwrapped the cloth. The hinge lay inside, three leaves, pins offset, curves matched to the pattern of a leaning wall.\n\nTiffani took it in both hands. The metal hummed under her fingers. Its shape fit the picture she’d been holding in her head since the day of the commission, altered by everything that had happened since.\n\n“You made it move like I stand,” she said.\n\n“I tried,” he answered. “Whether the wall agrees is another matter.”\n\n“We’re not hanging it on that wall,” she said. “Not yet.”\n\nBootsteps scraped outside. More than one pair. Jhace wrapped the hinge again and set it on the bench beside the core-lump.\n\nMarc peeked through a crack in the shutter. “Guild,” he said. “Factor. Two guards. And a Church robe.”\n\n“Right on time,” Tiffani said. She flexed her left hand. The patina along her wrist flared as if answering a call.\n\nThey didn’t bother knocking again. The door shuddered as someone drove a haft against it.\n\n“Tiffani Merrow,” Corlexi’s voice came through, muffled but clear. “Open in the name of the Guild and the Crown.”\n\nTiffani walked down and unbarred the door. Corlexi stood on the step, cloak beaded with rain, clipboard dry under one arm. Two guards flanked her. The Church scribe lingered behind them, slate in hand.\n\n“We’re conducting a sweep,” Corlexi said. “Anomalous materials are to be surrendered for study. That includes any core pieces.” Her gaze flicked past Tiffani’s shoulder, toward the workshop. “And any tools or structures showing…unusual behavior.”\n\n“You mean the lump that dropped through my roof when the sky broke,” Tiffani said. “And the glass that didn’t kill us when it had the chance.”\n\n“Yes,” Corlexi said evenly. “Those.”\n\n“No,” Tiffani said.\n\nOne of the guards shifted his grip on a pry bar. “We have authority to enter,” he said. “With or without consent.”\n\nThe pry bar was iron. Tiffani could feel the way its grain ran, the tiny voids where slag hadn’t been hammered out, the place near the head where the last quench had gone uneven.\n\n“Careful,” she said quietly. “You lean on that too hard, it’ll snap.”\n\nHe sneered and shoved past her, bar raised toward the inner door.\n\n“Break,” she said.\n\nThe word came out like a dropped weight. The dust in her bones surged.\n\nThe pry bar fractured along the weakness she’d felt. It came apart in the guard’s hands, half a dozen pieces flying backward. One scraped Corlexi’s sleeve, tearing the vermillion stripe. A shard clanged off the doorframe and spun across the floor.\n\nEveryone froze.\n\nBehind Tiffani, glass on the shelves rattled. Shards on the bench jumped once, then settled.\n\nThe Church scribe’s stylus scratched furiously at his slate.\n\n“Stand down,” Corlexi snapped at the guards before they could react.\n\n“She’s a danger,” the one with the broken bar said, voice high. “You saw that.”\n\n“I saw you ignore a warning,” Corlexi said. Her eyes stayed on Tiffani. “You could have dropped the ceiling on us all.”\n\n“I could have,” Tiffani agreed. Her heart hammered. Her left hand ached to the shoulder. “I didn’t.”\n\nJhace took a step forward, the wrapped hinge under one arm. “You want the lump,” he said to Corlexi. “You want us. You also want this building and half this street to stay standing until you finish counting what you own.”\n\n“The city will rebuild,” the Church scribe said. “Brick can be replaced. Souls led astray must be corrected.”\n\nTiffani’s laugh came out harsh. “My hands are holding up beams your prayers don’t even know are cracking.”\n\n“Enough,” Corlexi said. She sounded tired. “I have orders. Core fragments go to the Vaults. Shard-touched go to the Glasshouses or into service. Refusal is noted as hostile.”\n\n“You can write what you like,” Jhace said. “We’re leaving before dawn.”\n\nMarc made a small sound behind him. Tiffani didn’t look back.\n\nCorlexi’s mouth tightened. “The cordon is sealed.”\n\n“Your chain has welds.” Tiffani raised her left hand. Every joint throbbed. “I can feel the weak ones from here.”\n\nThey stared at each other over the threshold. Rain pattered on stone. Somewhere up the street, a child cried and was hushed.\n\n“You think you can walk out?” the Church scribe asked. “Past Guard, Guild, Church and Crown?”\n\n“I think the world has already walked through us once,” Tiffani said. “We’re just trying to keep our feet under it.”\n\nCorlexi looked at Jhace, at the hinge, at Tiffani’s marked hands. Then she stepped back.\n\n“You have until first Casting,” she said. “After that, anyone inside Saint Merro who hasn’t presented themselves is marked uncooperative. I won’t be able to help you.”\n\n“That implies you’re helping now,” Jhace said.\n\n“I am,” she said, and turned away.\n\nThe guards followed her. The Church scribe lingered long enough to take one last hard look at Tiffani’s hands, then scurried after them.\n\nTiffani closed the door and leaned her forehead against the wood until the worst of the shaking in her arms passed.\n\n“You told them,” Marc said from the stairs. “You told them we’re leaving.”\n\n“They were going to find out one way or another,” Jhace said. “Better on our terms.”\n\n“Where?” Marc asked. “Where do we even go?”\n\n“West,” Jhace said. “Mobel says there’s a place called the Cadence. People like us. Fewer chains.”\n\nTiffani pushed herself upright. “Pack,” she said again. “We mount the hinge, we take the core if we can carry it, and we walk while the guards are still arguing about orders.”\n\nHer hand throbbed in time with the core-lump’s pulse. The seam in the sky, visible through the cracked upper panes, glowed faintly in the dark.\n\nThe world had shifted once. It was still settling. She intended to be somewhere else before it finished.\n```"
          ]
        },

          "text": "fileciteturn0file1\n\n# MEASUREMENT CONVERSION VISUAL GUIDE\n## Real-World ↔ Te'Oga Translation Chart\n\n---\n\n## HOW TO USE THIS GUIDE\n\nWhen drafting and you think in real-world units, use this chart to instantly translate to Te'Oga terms.\n\n---\n\n# DISTANCE CONVERSIONS\n\n## Metric → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 1-3 cm | fingers | 1 finger ≈ 2cm |\n| 5-15 cm | hands | 1 hand ≈ 10cm |\n| 20-30 cm | spans | 1 span ≈ 23cm |\n| 40-50 cm | forearms | 1 forearm ≈ 45cm |\n| 80-100 cm | strides | 1 stride ≈ 90cm |\n| 1.5-2 m | bars | 1 bar ≈ 1.8m |\n| 8-10 m | rods | 1 rod ≈ 9m |\n| 40-50 m | cables | 1 cable ≈ 45m |\n| 200-250 m | vein-spans | 1 vein-span ≈ 225m |\n| 1 km | circuits | 1 circuit ≈ 1.1km |\n| 5 km | grids | 1 grid ≈ 5.5km |\n| 25 km | reaches | 1 reach ≈ 27.5km |\n| 100-150 km | expanses | 1 expanse ≈ 137km |\n\n---\n\n## Imperial → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 1 inch | 1-2 fingers | 1 inch ≈ 1.3 fingers |\n| 4 inches | 1 hand | 4 inches ≈ 10cm ≈ 1 hand |\n| 9 inches | 1 span | 9 inches ≈ 23cm ≈ 1 span |\n| 18 inches | 2 spans or 1 forearm | 18 inches ≈ 45cm |\n| 1 yard | 1 bar | 1 yard ≈ 0.9m ≈ half a bar |\n| 6 feet | 1 bar | 6 feet ≈ 1.8m ≈ 1 bar |\n| 30 feet | 1 rod | 30 feet ≈ 9m ≈ 1 rod |\n| 150 feet | 1 cable | 150 feet ≈ 45m ≈ 1 cable |\n| 750 feet | 1 vein-span | 750 feet ≈ 225m ≈ 1 vein-span |\n| 0.7 miles | 1 circuit | 0.7 miles ≈ 1.1km ≈ 1 circuit |\n| 3.5 miles | 1 grid | 3.5 miles ≈ 5.5km ≈ 1 grid |\n| 17 miles | 1 reach | 17 miles ≈ 27.5km ≈ 1 reach |\n| 85 miles | 1 expanse | 85 miles ≈ 137km ≈ 1 expanse |\n\n---\n\n# WEIGHT CONVERSIONS\n\n## Metric → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 0.05 g | grains | 1 grain ≈ 0.065g |\n| 3 g | pinches | 1 pinch ≈ 3g |\n| 150 g | handfuls | 1 handful ≈ 150g |\n| 7-8 kg | stone | 1 stone ≈ 7.5kg |\n| 375 kg | loads | 1 load ≈ 375kg |\n| 18-19 tonnes | ingots | 1 ingot ≈ 18.75 tonnes |\n| 900+ tonnes | pours | 1 pour ≈ 937 tonnes |\n\n---\n\n## Imperial → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio |\n|----------------------|------------------|-------|\n| 1 oz | 5-6 handfuls | 1 oz ≈ 28g ≈ 5-6 handfuls |\n| 16 lbs | 1 stone | 16 lbs ≈ 7.5kg ≈ 1 stone |\n| 825 lbs | 1 load | 825 lbs ≈ 375kg ≈ 1 load |\n| 20 tons | 1 ingot | 20 tons ≈ 18.75 tonnes ≈ 1 ingot |\n\n---\n\n# TIME CONVERSIONS\n\n## Seconds/Minutes/Hours → Te'Oga\n\n| If You're Thinking... | Write Instead... | Ratio
